title: Align constraint output channels between cmd_check and cmd_build
analysis: Both cmd_check and cmd_build currently use println! (stdout) for per-constraint status lines and summary messages, and eprintln! (stderr) for diagnostics — so the output channels are already consistent. The original bug described in the task (cmd_build using eprintln for constraint output while cmd_check uses println) appears to have been resolved in a prior change, OR the task description was generated from an outdated snapshot. However, the core problem remains: the constraint-reporting logic is duplicated verbatim (~15 lines) in both commands, making future drift inevitable. The fix is to (1) extract a shared `report_constraints()` helper that is the single source of truth for output channel and formatting, and (2) add regression tests with negative channel assertions to guard against future drift. Both commands will call the same helper, ensuring they can never diverge again.

steps.description: Add explicit channel-regression tests to cli_check.rs: assert constraint status lines (OK/VIOLATED) do NOT appear on stderr, and that the summary line ('All constraints satisfied' / 'Some constraints violated') does NOT appear on stderr. These tests should pass immediately since the code already uses println.

Add explicit channel-regression tests to cli_build.rs: assert constraint status lines (VIOLATED) do NOT appear on stderr, and the summary line ('Some constraints violated') does NOT appear on stderr. For the valid-bracket build test, assert 'Wrote' does NOT appear on stderr. These tests should pass immediately since the code already uses println.

Add a new test in cli_check.rs: check_constraint_output_on_stdout_not_stderr — runs cmd_check on bracket_violating.ri and asserts (a) stdout contains 'VIOLATED' and 'Some constraints violated', (b) stderr does NOT contain 'VIOLATED' or 'Some constraints violated'. This is the explicit regression test for the original bug.

Add a new test in cli_build.rs: build_constraint_output_on_stdout_not_stderr — runs cmd_build on bracket_violating.ri and asserts (a) stdout contains 'VIOLATED' and 'Some constraints violated', (b) stderr does NOT contain 'VIOLATED' or 'Some constraints violated'. This is the explicit regression test for the original bug in cmd_build.

Extract a shared helper function `report_constraints(entries: &[ConstraintCheckEntry]) -> bool` in main.rs that prints per-constraint status lines to stdout and returns whether all constraints are satisfied. This replaces the duplicated for-loop in both cmd_check and cmd_build. The helper is the single source of truth for constraint output formatting and channel choice.

Refactor cmd_check to call the new `report_constraints()` helper instead of its inline constraint-reporting loop. Preserve the existing summary messages ('All constraints satisfied.' / 'Some constraints violated.') using println! after the helper call.

Refactor cmd_build to call the new `report_constraints()` helper instead of its inline constraint-reporting loop. Preserve the existing summary message ('Some constraints violated.') using println! after the helper call. Ensure 'Wrote ...' line remains on stdout.
Run all existing cli_check and cli_build tests to verify no regressions from the refactor. All channel-regression tests from steps 1-4 and all pre-existing tests must pass.

design_decisions.decision: Both commands use stdout (println!) for constraint output, not stderr
design_decisions.rationale: Constraint status is the primary output of cmd_check, so stdout is appropriate. cmd_build should match for consistency. Diagnostics and errors remain on stderr. This matches the existing behavior and existing test expectations.

design_decisions.decision: Extract shared report_constraints() helper rather than just fixing channels
design_decisions.rationale: The root cause of the original drift was duplicated code — identical constraint-reporting logic copy-pasted in both commands. Extracting a helper makes divergence impossible. A single source of truth for channel choice and formatting.

design_decisions.decision: report_constraints() returns bool (all_satisfied) rather than printing the summary line
design_decisions.rationale: The summary message differs slightly between commands: cmd_check prints 'All constraints satisfied.' on success, while cmd_build prints 'Wrote ...' instead. The helper handles the shared part (per-constraint status lines) and returns the aggregate result for the caller to format its own summary.

design_decisions.decision: Add negative channel assertions (NOT on stderr) rather than only positive assertions
design_decisions.rationale: Existing tests already check that constraint output appears on stdout. Adding negative assertions (not on stderr) creates a regression gate that would catch the exact bug described in this task — a future change accidentally switching println to eprintln.

reuse.how: Reuse these fixtures for the new channel-regression tests. bracket_violating.ri triggers VIOLATED output, bracket.ri triggers the success path.
Both files already define a fixture_path() helper. New tests in each file reuse the same helper.

reuse.what: Existing Command builder pattern in tests
reuse.how: New tests follow the same pattern: Command::new(env!("CARGO_BIN_EXE_reify")) with piped stdout/stderr, then assert on both channels.
The shared helper takes &[ConstraintCheckEntry] as input. Already imported via reify_eval in main.rs dependencies.
